problem_summary: 2023 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability / light pollution). The team had to: (1) build a broadly-applicable indicator measuring light-pollution risk at any location, (2) apply it to four distinct site types, (3) propose three intervention strategies with implementation steps and effects, (4) apply interventions to two sites and pick the best per site, and (5) produce a public-facing flyer. This O-award paper answers all five via two model families: an LPA (Light Pollution Assessment) model that quantifies impact and computes a risk factor Ω, and an LPI (Light Pollution Intervene) model that scores three intervention strategies using coupling-coordination analysis. It self-collected UK data (the problem provided none).
models_used: LPA Model I - Pollution Quantification: a 3-tier indicator system (Human / Society / Ecology) with 13 indicators, split into Q_positive (=GDP per capita) and Q_negative (weighted sum of 12 negative indicators)

EWM-AHP hybrid weighting: Entropy Weight Method (objective, data-driven) applied within each dimension to set secondary-indicator weights, plus Analytic Hierarchy Process (subjective judgment matrix, CR=0.0053) to weight the three first-level dimensions delta=(0.1283,0.2764,0.5954)
Treanor model (mechanistic optics) for the Environmental Pollution indicator EP = L(r)/L_N = (A/r + B/r^2)e^(-kr), with observation constants A,B proportional to urban population
Custom biodiversity indicator DS = (B_total/Area)*BR*gamma summed over vertebrate/invertebrate classes (species density times a light-impact factor gamma)

Model II - Risk Assessment: ALARP criterion (Broad-Acceptable / Reasonably-Acceptable / Unacceptable zones) combined with Cost-Risk analysis, operationalized as a custom risk factor Omega = standardized Q_negative / standardized Q_positive, extended with growth-rate terms where C_negative is assumed normally distributed (expectation ~6%)

Pearson correlation as a validation device (0.8002 between normalized light intensity and computed negative impact over 10 random regions; also 0.85293 between electricity consumption and light pollution to justify the RC indicator)

Model III - LPI intervention dynamics: three time-evolution strategies. Resource-Friendly (curfew): RC(t)=RC_0(1-E)^t for resource decay, CR(t)=CR_0(1+r_CR)^t plus a logistic crime-reduction term. Environment-Friendly: AOD air-quality reduction lowering night-sky brightness ~3.2x. Human-Friendly: spectral shift / wavelength ban with PHS(t)=PHS_0(1+r_PHS)^t

Coupling Coordination Degree model: C_LPA (coupling) = cube-root of product/sum^3 of the three dimension scores, T_LPA (coordination) = weighted sum, D_LPA = sqrt(C*T), mapped to a 10-level coordination scale to compare pre/post-intervention system balance
Sensitivity analysis: perturbing the Treanor distance parameter r by +/-5% and observing EP stability across six regions
key_techniques: Splitting the impact into Q_positive and Q_negative so light's dual nature (economic benefit vs. health/eco harm) is modeled explicitly rather than treated as pure harm - this directly enabled a ratio-based risk factor Omega

Combining objective (EWM) and subjective (AHP) weighting to neutralize each method's weakness - EWM's sample-dependence and AHP's subjectivity - and reporting the AHP consistency ratio (0.0053) to prove the judgment matrix is valid
Borrowing a domain-specific physical model (Treanor) for one indicator instead of inventing a black box, giving the EP term physical credibility
Using Pearson correlation as cheap, persuasive external validation against an independent proxy (raw light brightness) with a stated threshold (>0.8 = strong)
Mapping a custom continuous risk index Omega onto a recognized risk framework (ALARP) with explicit numeric thresholds (0-0.35 / 0.35-0.9 / >0.9), turning a number into an actionable decision rule
Coupling-coordination analysis to judge interventions not by raw score gains but by whether the three subsystems become more balanced - a sophisticated, sustainability-appropriate lens
Site-typed case study design (Protected / Rural / Suburban / Urban by population density) so one indicator visibly discriminates across the four required site types
Per-site differentiated recommendations (Rutland -> Human-Friendly +10% health; Westminster -> Resource-Friendly curfew 50.0080->59.6481) showing the model yields non-trivial, site-specific answers
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness, credibility, and decision-relevance. It answers every one of the five sub-tasks explicitly and visibly, including the often-skimped flyer. The indicator system is principled (3 tiers, 13 indicators distilled from 73 candidate official indicators, >300 data points each) rather than ad hoc, and the EWM+AHP hybrid is exactly the kind of defensible weighting judges reward while pre-empting the 'your weights are arbitrary' critique. Crucially it validates (Pearson 0.8002 against an independent light-brightness proxy) and stress-tests (sensitivity on r). The risk factor Omega is the paper's standout: a single, transferable, threshold-mapped number grounded in the recognized ALARP framework - it gives the judge a clean takeaway. The coupling-coordination layer elevates the intervention analysis above a naive 'higher score = better,' which reads as genuine sustainability thinking. Finally the per-site differentiated conclusions (rural wants Human-Friendly, urban wants curfew) prove the model produces specific, intuitive, actionable answers rather than a uniform recommendation."
section_highlights: "Structure is textbook-MCM and easy for a tired judge to navigate: Summary states all three models and headline numbers up front; a labeled flow chart (Figure 3) maps Part 1 prep -> Part 2 LPA/LPI -> Part 3 results; Assumptions are each paired with an explicit Explanation; a Notations table precedes the math. The literature review is unusually strong - it classifies prior work along three axes (composition / data-space 2D-vs-3D / macro-vs-micro effects) and uses that taxonomy to justify the team's own scoping choices (night-only, mostly-2D data). Visual storytelling is heavy and effective: night-Earth hook quote from a TED talk, UK choropleth maps for site selection, stacked-bar dimension scores per community, and clear before/after intervention bar charts (Figures 11-12). The model-naming (LPA/LPI, risk factor Omega) gives the work a memorable identity. Strengths/Weaknesses section is honest. The flyer is genuinely public-facing (icon-driven, curfew call-to-action, benefits list)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many parameters are asserted rather than derived or sourced - alpha/beta in RC=EC*alpha/beta are never given numerically; the 6% normal-distribution expectation, r_PHS values (12.84% rural, 9.65% urban), 8.5% LED reduction, and curfew efficiency E are plugged in with thin justification

Omega's thresholds (0.35, 0.9) are attributed to a single reference [10] and mapped onto ALARP without derivation - the boundaries are effectively hand-set, so the headline risk classifications (Suburban 1.04, Urban 1.27 = 'Unacceptable') rest on chosen cutoffs

Validation is correlational only: Pearson 0.8002 against raw light brightness partly tests that 'more light => more computed harm,' which is somewhat built into the construction (DS/EP scale with brightness), risking circularity rather than independent ground truth
Q_positive is reduced to a single variable (GDP per capita), so the entire 'benefit' side of the risk ratio hinges on one indicator while the harm side has 12 - an asymmetry that can distort Omega
The Treanor constants A,B are calibrated for Italy and applied to the UK; r is fixed at 10 (or 1.3 km elsewhere) and city treated as a square - strong simplifications acknowledged only lightly
Sensitivity analysis perturbs only one parameter (distance r, +/-5%) on one indicator (EP); weights, thresholds, and the normal-distribution assumption are never sensitivity-tested
Some data provenance is loose - the electricity/light Pearson uses Chinese provincial cities while the study country is the UK; unit/standardization details (species per km^2) are sketchy
Self-admitted weaknesses: assumes full government/public cooperation with interventions, and forces sudden shocks (disasters) into a smooth normally-distributed change rate
reusable_patterns: Positive/negative impact split feeding a ratio-based risk index (Omega = Q_neg/Q_pos): a clean, transferable template for any 'double-edged' sustainability factor (tourism, energy, urbanization)

EWM (objective, within-tier) + AHP (subjective, across-tier) hybrid weighting with a reported AHP consistency ratio - a default, judge-pleasing recipe for multi-indicator scoring, with the EWM/AHP complementarity explicitly argued in the Strengths section
Anchor a custom continuous index onto a recognized standard with numeric thresholds (here ALARP zones) so the number maps to an action - reusable for any risk/severity output
Validate a constructed index by correlating it (Pearson, stated threshold) against an independent observable proxy, then sensitivity-test a key parameter - a fast two-step credibility booster

Coupling Coordination Degree model (C, T, D with a 10-level scale) to evaluate whether an intervention balances subsystems, not just raises a score - ideal for any multi-dimension sustainability trade-off

Site-typing the case study along one driver (population density -> Protected/Rural/Suburban/Urban) so a single indicator visibly discriminates across required scenarios and yields differentiated recommendations
Distill a large candidate set down transparently (73 official indicators -> 10 + 3 ecological) and state data volume (>300 points/indicator) to signal rigor
Borrow a discipline-specific mechanistic model (Treanor optics) for one sub-indicator instead of a black box, lending physical credibility

Document structure scaffold: Summary with named models + headline numbers, work flow chart, paired Assumption/Explanation list, Notations table, taxonomy-driven literature review, and a real public-facing flyer as the final deliverable
